name: Gravel tour around Peña Soto
name_original: Vuelta gravel a la peña Soto
description:
With only 37.4 kilometers and 1,000 meters of accumulated positive elevation gain, this is a demanding Route, marked by two major ascents. The first and most important one surrounds the Peña Soto, an enormous mass of conglomerate that houses an important colony of vultures on its steep walls. The second ascent starts from Albelda in the direction of Clavijo and marks the final descent to Ribafrecha, the origin and end of the Route.

The Route begins in Ribafrecha, in the middle of the alluvial plain of the Leza River, an area of abundant dryland crops characterized by forming intricate mosaics. With Mount Laturce always on your left, you advance across the plain, first crossing La Unión, until you head south to gradually climb up to Peña Soto.

Before you go through Clavijo, whose imposing castle looks out into the void from the rock of the same name. In Clavijo it is a good idea to get supplies, since until Nalda, the next town on the Route, you will not find any type of service.

After Clavijo you continue going up tracks surrounded by pine forests, until you almost surround the Soto rock. After the descent to Nalda, its castle awaits you, which crowns the town right in its center. From Nalda to Albelda de Iregua, you pedal along the Iregua Roman road, which runs along the river that gives it its name.

From Albelda a short ascent begins, barely three kilometers, but intense. The climb leads to Clavijo, but you leave it at the Cuatro Caminos crossroads. There you turn left to take a paved path that takes you back to La Unión. From there you take a dirt track gently downhill to Ribafrecha, the end of the Route.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Ribafrecha offers a diverse landscape for riders, characterized by rolling hills, historical paths, and areas around reservoirs. The region features varied terrain, including routes that pass by ancient Roman roads and significant landmarks like Clavijo Castle. Riders can expect a mix of open country, some forested sections, and routes that follow river valleys. Best gravel bike trails around Ribafrecha The most popular gravel bike trail is Gravel tour around Peña Soto, a 22.8 miles (36.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 54 minutes to complete, featuring significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Clavijo Castle – Iregua Roman Road loop from Logroño, a difficult 33.0 miles (53.2 km) path that explores historical routes and varied terrain. Local gravel bikers also love the Iregua Roman Road – Iregua Park loop from Albelda de Iregua, a 26.9 miles (43.3 km) trail leading through river valleys and parkland, often completed in about 3 hours 3 minutes. Gravel biking around Ribafrecha is defined by historical Roman roads, river valleys, and rolling hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult routes. The routes in Ribafrecha are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 200 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Ribafrecha's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many gravel bike trails are available around Ribafrecha?
A: There are &lt;strong&gt;over 7 gravel bike routes&lt;/strong&gt; documented around Ribafrecha on komoot, offering a variety of experiences for different skill levels. The komoot community has explored these trails over 200 times.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on gravel bike trails in Ribafrecha?
A: Gravel biking around Ribafrecha features a diverse landscape of &lt;strong&gt;rolling hills, historical paths, and areas around reservoirs&lt;/strong&gt;. You'll encounter a mix of open country, some forested sections, and routes that follow river valleys, often including ancient Roman roads.

Q: Are there any beginner-friendly gravel bike routes in Ribafrecha?
A: While many routes are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easier option available. For a less strenuous ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1050757427/gravel-tour-around-pena-soto" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gravel tour around Peña Soto&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a good introduction to the region's gravel paths.

Q: Where can I find challenging gravel bike routes for advanced riders?
A: Advanced riders seeking a challenge will find several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23741368" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Clavijo Castle – Iregua Roman Road loop from Logroño&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 33.0-mile (53.2 km) path exploring historical routes and varied terrain. Another difficult option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23742089" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Grajera Reservoir – La Grajera Park loop from Logroño&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 68 km.

Q: Are there any circular gravel bike routes around Ribafrecha?
A: Yes, many of the gravel bike routes in the region are designed as loops. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31022772" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iregua Roman Road – Iregua Park loop from Albelda de Iregua&lt;/a&gt; is a popular 26.9-mile (43.3 km) circular trail that takes you through river valleys and parkland. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31029057" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San Miguel Park – La Grajera Reservoir loop from Logroño&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What historical landmarks or attractions can I see along the gravel bike trails?
A: The region is rich in history. Many routes pass by significant landmarks such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2706737" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Clavijo Castle&lt;/a&gt;, a prominent historical site. You can also explore areas like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5338196" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iregua Park&lt;/a&gt; and the ancient Roman roads that crisscross the landscape.

Q: What do other gravel bikers enjoy most about the trails in Ribafrecha?
A: The komoot community highly rates the gravel biking experience in Ribafrecha, with an average score of &lt;strong&gt;4.6 stars from over 10 reviews&lt;/strong&gt;. Riders often praise the diverse terrain, the historical context of the routes, and the mix of open country and river valley paths.

Q: Are there any reservoirs or water bodies accessible by gravel bike in the area?
A: Yes, several routes incorporate reservoirs into their paths. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23742089" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Grajera Reservoir – La Grajera Park loop from Logroño&lt;/a&gt; is one such route that takes you around a significant water body, offering scenic views.

Q: What is the best time of year to go gravel biking in Ribafrecha?
A: While specific seasonal data isn't provided, the varied terrain and historical paths suggest that &lt;strong&gt;spring and autumn&lt;/strong&gt; would likely offer the most pleasant conditions, with milder temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summer can be warm, and winter might bring colder, potentially wetter conditions, especially in higher elevations.

Q: Are there any notable peaks or viewpoints accessible from the gravel bike trails?
A: While the routes primarily focus on rolling hills and valleys, the region is surrounded by several peaks. You might catch glimpses of summits like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4054053" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monte La Pila&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6168149" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico de la Sala&lt;/a&gt;, which add to the scenic backdrop of your ride.

Q: Are there any specific areas known for their natural beauty or forested sections?
A: Yes, the region includes forested sections and areas of natural beauty. &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5338196" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Iregua Park&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, is a highlight that features prominently in some routes, offering a greener, more secluded riding experience along the river valley.
tips: The town of Clavijo is located in the heart of Rioja's Tierra de Cameros, in a very broken terrain, surrounded by mountains with forests of holm oaks and oaks, Clavijo emerges, a place with a lot of legend and history. Barely 16 kilometers from Logroño, its fortress watches over from its battlements the roads that connect Castilla, Navarra and Aragón, the Leza river gorge and the Iregua and Ebro river valleys.

Splendid castle in the process of restoration, it is worth the climb. The date of construction is not clear but it seems to have been in the 13th century. In the 19th century it suffered abandonment and plunder, its stones being used for other constructions in the area.

This ascent by paved road is 2.4 kilometers long, from the exit of the town of Albelda de Iregua to the crossroads called Los Cuatro Caminos, from where you can continue up to Clavijo, or take the royal glen to La Unión.

It is a tough climb, with an average percentage of 9.3%.

Small hermitage built at the beginning of the 13th century. It was the parish church of the disappeared town of Palazuelos, depopulated in the mid-14th century during the black plague epidemic that devastated Europe.

This church, built in 1965 by the architect and priest Gerardo Cuadra, is considered one of the most important architectural monuments of the 20th century.

It is a rural church in which special attention has been paid to the prioritization of spaces and volumes, light elements and their integration with the rural environment.

The main space is reserved for the celebration of worship and is designed so that interest is concentrated on the presbytery and specifically on the altar. The trapezoidal shape of the plant, the roofs and the cylindrical lantern concentrate interest on the altar as a "fundamental liturgical center".

waypoints.name: Ascent from Albelda to Cuatro Caminos
Hermitage of Santa Fe de Palazuelos
